---
title: "Doc 1 Presentations & Peer Evaluation"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-level: 2
    center: true
    slide-number: true
    theme: simple
    width: 1600
    height: 1200
    preview-links: auto
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
---

# Week 11: Final Push

## What We Are Doing Today

- Quick recap: visualization skills from last week
- Quick orientation: Colab, Anaconda, IDEs, and coding basics
- Peer-evaluation walkthrough
- Deadline update: final Doc 1 submission
- Finish any leftover Professional Discovery presentations
- Begin Doc 1 final presentations

The goal is not to add another full lecture. The goal is to make your Doc 1 argument clearer, more visual, and easier for peers to evaluate fairly.

## Deadline Update: Final Doc 1

To give everyone time to revise after presentations and peer feedback:

- **Doc 1 draft / presentation version:** used for the Apr 21 and Apr 28 presentation sessions
- **Final Doc 1 submission:** due **Tuesday, May 5, 2026, by 12:00 PM noon**

This is a generous revision window. Use it to tighten the argument, improve figures, clarify methods, and respond to the most useful feedback you receive.

## Presentation Timing

Each Doc 1 presentation gets:

- **5 minutes** for the presentation
- **1 minute** after each speaker for peer evaluation
- Remaining presentations continue next class

With two class sessions, 5 minutes is reasonable. Do not try to cover every detail of the paper. Present the argument clearly enough that a peer can understand the question, evidence, method, insight, and implication.

## What Fits in 5 Minutes?

Use this structure if you are unsure:

| Time | Focus | What to say |
|---:|---|---|
| 0:00-0:45 | Question | What are you investigating, and why does it matter? |
| 0:45-1:45 | Evidence base | What literature, cases, or context shaped your thinking? |
| 1:45-2:45 | Method | What did you collect or analyze, and why was it appropriate? |
| 2:45-4:15 | Main finding | What is the most important insight? Show one strong visual if possible. |
| 4:15-5:00 | So what | What does this imply for design, innovation, or future work? |

# Visualization Recap

## Last Week's Core Skill

Data visualization is not decoration. It is a way to make an argument visible.

Ask:

- What comparison am I trying to show?
- What pattern, distribution, flow, or relationship matters?
- What should the reader understand faster because of this figure?
- Is the visual simpler than the paragraph it replaces?

## Choosing the Right Visual

| If your point is... | Use... |
|---|---|
| Comparing categories | Bar chart |
| Showing spread or variation | Histogram or box plot |
| Showing relationships across variables | Scatter plot or heatmap |
| Showing parts of a whole | Stacked bar, treemap, or pie chart if very simple |
| Showing movement or transformation | Sankey diagram or flow diagram |
| Showing themes in text | Thematic table, quote matrix, or word cloud |
| Showing geography | Map or choropleth, only if place matters |

## Static vs Interactive

- **Static figures** are best for Doc 1: clear, printable, and easy to cite.
- **Plotly** is useful when hover labels, maps, treemaps, or Sankey diagrams help explain the data.
- **D3.js** is for custom web-native storytelling. Powerful, but not the fastest path for most Doc 1 drafts.
- **Flourish, Tableau, and Power BI** are practical if you want interactive visuals without writing much code.

Rule of thumb: start static. Add interaction only when the interaction teaches something.

## Mermaid: Diagrams Without Design Software

Mermaid lets you write diagrams in Markdown-like text. It is useful for process maps, research workflows, decision trees, and concept relationships.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  RQ[Research question] --> Evidence[Evidence]
  Evidence --> Method[Method]
  Method --> Finding[Finding]
  Finding --> Implication[Design implication]
```

Good use in Doc 1: show your research logic, not decorative complexity.

# Tooling Recap

## Colab, Anaconda, IDEs

| Tool | What it is | Best use |
|---|---|---|
| Google Colab | Browser-based Jupyter notebook | Fastest way to run Python without setup |
| Jupyter Notebook / JupyterLab | Notebook interface for code, text, and figures | Reproducible analysis and teaching demos |
| Anaconda | Local Python distribution and environment manager | Running notebooks locally with installed packages |
| IDE | Coding workspace such as VS Code or Spyder | Larger projects, file management, debugging |

For this course, Colab is enough. Anaconda and IDEs matter if you want a more permanent local research workflow.

## Coding Basics for Data Visualization

Most notebook visualizations follow the same pattern:

```python
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("data/survey_data.csv")
summary = df.groupby("group")["score"].mean().reset_index()

sns.barplot(data=summary, x="group", y="score")
plt.title("Average score by group")
plt.show()
```

The important idea: load data, summarize what matters, then choose a chart that matches the claim.

## What Your Figure Needs

Every figure should answer these four questions:

- What am I looking at?
- What do the axes, colors, or sizes mean?
- What pattern should I notice?
- Why does this matter for the research question?

If a figure cannot answer those questions, revise it or remove it.

# Peer Evaluation

## Why Peer Evaluation Counts

Peer evaluation is 20% of the course because evaluating research is itself a research skill.

You are not judging personality, accent, confidence, or whether you personally like the topic. You are judging whether the research argument comes through clearly and fairly.

## How We Record It

Use the private Google Form after each speaker.

- Submit one response per presenter
- Do not evaluate your own presentation
- Scores are not shown live
- The required comment should help the presenter revise Doc 1

Paper notes are optional. The Google Form is the official peer-evaluation record.

## Peer Evaluation Form

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

Scan this after each Doc 1 presenter:

- Submit one response per presenter
- Keep the form open during presentations
- Use the same QR code for every speaker
- Direct link: <https://forms.gle/r1MMRVJQ3rRr4sFXA>

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

![](data/doc1_peer_eval_qr.png){fig-alt="QR code linking to the DESN2003 Doc 1 Final Presentation Peer Evaluation Google Form." width=80%}

:::

::::

## The 5 Criteria

| # | Criterion | Listen for |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sharp problem statement | Clear, focused question that matters |
| 2 | Evidence integration | Literature and data support one argument |
| 3 | Method and tool fit | Methods are appropriate and justified |
| 4 | Narrative flow and insight | Findings lead to a clear implication |
| 5 | Delivery and visuals | Slides or visuals help rather than distract |

Use the full 0-5 range, but be fair.

## Score Anchors

| Score | Meaning |
|---:|---|
| 0 | Missing or not enough information to evaluate |
| 1 | Not yet clear |
| 2 | Partly visible, but major gaps remain |
| 3 | Understandable, but needs clearer evidence or structure |
| 4 | Strong, with minor areas to improve |
| 5 | Very clear, convincing, and well supported |

Do not give everyone the same score. Also do not punish someone for researching a difficult or unfamiliar topic.

## Required Comment

After each presentation, write one useful comment:

- "The strongest part of this research argument was..."
- "One thing that would make the Doc 1 draft clearer is..."

Avoid vague comments like "good job" or "confusing." Say what worked, what was unclear, and why.

# Presentation Checklist

## Before You Present

- State the research question early
- Show one clear visual, table, diagram, or evidence summary if possible
- Explain method fit, not just method choice
- Make the "so what" explicit
- End with one limitation or next step

## While Listening

For each presenter, ask:

- Can I repeat their research question?
- Do I understand what evidence they used?
- Do I understand why their method fits?
- Can I identify their main finding?
- Do I understand the implication?

If the answer is no, your comment should help them locate that gap.

## Today Is a Draft Moment

This is not a performance contest. It is a structured way to test whether the Doc 1 argument is legible to another reader.

Good peer review helps the presenter revise. Good presenting helps the peer reviewer understand what rigorous research communication looks like.
